title: Signal Dry Bulk Weekly Report
summary:
Main
The last days of March are trending a softer momentum in dry bulk freight rates with Russian geopolitical uncertainty having a significant influence on grain trading. Germany's largest agricultural trader BayWa confirmed last week that wheat exports from Ukraine and Russia are still being blocked by Russia from leaving the Black Sea. The Panamanian Maritime Authority also revealed that the Russian Navy was preventing 200 to 300 vessels from departing from the Black Sea, most of them transporting grain, while other reports suggested that around 100 vessels were blocked.

children.title: Main
children.summary: The last days of March are trending a softer momentum in dry bulk freight rates with Russian geopolitical uncertainty having a significant influence on grain trading. Germany's largest agricultural trader BayWa confirmed last week that wheat exports from Ukraine and Russia are still being blocked by Russia from leaving the Black Sea.

children.title: SECTION 1 - FREIGHT - Market Rates ($/t) - Weaker
children.summary:
‘The Big Picture' - Capesize and Panamax Bulkers and Smaller Ship Sizes
Image reference: ch1.png | assets/2022-03-23_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_ch1_dde6a69e6644.png
Capesize Brazil to NChina rates sustained levels around $29/t one week before the end of March, almost at the same levels from the previous week, with a soft declining sentiment due to Chinese economic instability from the rising COVID-19 cases. Market rates remain remarkably higher than the low of Week 3 $18/t.

children.title: Number of Vessels - Decreasing
children.summary:
Supply Trend Lines for Key Load Areas
Image reference: ch2.png | assets/2022-03-23_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_ch2_9944aa0c229c.png
The ballasters' view keeps a firm decrease in the panamax segment over the last three weeks, while the number of ballasters remains significantly high in the supramax segment. In the Capesize segment, the number of vessels sailing in ballast status has now dropped below the one-year average of 80 vessels, while in the Panamax, the number is now at one of the lowest levels of the last year at 76 vessels.

children.title: Decreasing
children.summary:
Image reference: ch3.png | assets/2022-03-23_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_ch3_9db3e73507d0.png
The last days of March confirm the upward evolution of demand ton days for the Panamax vessels, while the trend decreases for the smaller vessel sizes, supramax and Handysize. In the Capesize segment, we see a reversed sentiment this week towards a positive note despite the ongoing concerns for a weaker Chinese iron ore demand and signs of decrease last week.

children.title: Dry bulk ships congested around Chinese ports
section_path: Dry bulk ships congested around Chinese ports
children.section_path_text: Dry bulk ships congested around Chinese ports
children.summary:
Image reference: ch4.png | assets/2022-03-23_signal-dry-bulk-weekly-report_img_ch4_af7bbfb3c499.png
The number of ship congestion is on an increasing trend for four successive weeks. The levels have surpassed the minimum of 860 vessels since Week 8, while the overall trend is still below the record highs witnessed last quarter of the previous year.
